title: Colombia

infobox.value: [Creole English](/wiki/San_Andr%C3%A9s%E2%80%93Providencia_Creole "San Andrés–Providencia Creole") (in [San Andrés and Providencia](/wiki/San_Andr%C3%A9s_and_Providencia "San Andrés and Providencia"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) [64 other languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Colombia "Languages of Colombia")[[a]](#endnote_iboxa)

* 84.99% [Mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo_Colombians "Mestizo Colombians")-[White](/wiki/White_Colombians "White Colombians")[[a]](#cite_note-4) * 9.34% [Black](/wiki/Black_Colombians "Black Colombians") * 4.31% [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Colombia "Indigenous peoples in Colombia") * 0.01% [Romanis](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Colombia "Romani people in Colombia") * 1.35% not specified

* 70% [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Colombia "Catholic Church in Colombia") * 17% other [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Colombia "Christianity in Colombia") * 11% [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") * 1.9% other
1,141,748 km2 (440,831 sq mi) ([25th](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"))
Neutral increase 52,695,952[[6]](#cite_note-7) ([27th](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population"))
46.15/km2 (119.5/sq mi) ([174th](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density"))
Increase $19,770[[7]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.CO-8) ([82nd](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita"))
Increase 0.788[[9]](#cite_note-UNHDR-10) high ([83rd](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"))
*[Himno Nacional de la República de Colombia](/wiki/National_Anthem_of_Colombia "National Anthem of Colombia")* (Spanish) "National Anthem of the Republic of Colombia" | |

Although the Colombian Constitution specifies Spanish (*Castellano*) as the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") in all Colombian territory, other languages spoken in the country by ethnic groups – approximately 68 languages – each is also official in its territory.[[10]](#cite_note-11) English is also official in the [archipelago of San Andrés, Providencia and Santa Catalina](/wiki/Archipelago_of_San_Andr%C3%A9s,_Providencia_and_Santa_Catalina "Archipelago of San Andrés, Providencia and Santa Catalina").[[11]](#cite_note-LEY47DE1993-12) 2. **[^](#ref_iboxb)** The official Colombian time[[12]](#cite_note-13) is controlled and coordinated by the National Institute of Metrology.[[13]](#cite_note-14) | |

**Colombia**,[[b]](#cite_note-16) officially the **Republic of Colombia**,[[c]](#cite_note-17) is a country located in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), with [insular regions](/wiki/Insular_region_of_Colombia "Insular region of Colombia") in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). Colombia's mainland is bordered by the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea") to the north, [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") to the east, [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") to the southeast, [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") and [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") to the south and southwest, the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") to the west, and [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama") to the northwest. Colombia is divided into 32 [departments](/wiki/Departments_of_Colombia "Departments of Colombia"). The Capital District of [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá") is the [country's largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Colombia_by_population "List of cities in Colombia by population") hosting the main financial and cultural hub. Other urban areas include [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín"), [Cali](/wiki/Cali "Cali"), [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla"), [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia"), [Bucaramanga](/wiki/Bucaramanga "Bucaramanga"), [Pereira](/wiki/Pereira,_Colombia "Pereira, Colombia"), [Santa Marta](/wiki/Santa_Marta "Santa Marta"), [Cúcuta](/wiki/C%C3%BAcuta "Cúcuta"), [Ibagué](/wiki/Ibagu%C3%A9 "Ibagué"), [Villavicencio](/wiki/Villavicencio "Villavicencio") and [Manizales](/wiki/Manizales "Manizales"). It covers an area of 1,141,748 square kilometers (440,831 sq mi) and has a population of around 52 million. Its rich [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage")[[15]](#cite_note-18)—including language, religion, cuisine, and art—reflects its history as a colony, fusing cultural elements brought by [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Colombia "Immigration to Colombia") from Europe[[16]](#cite_note-19)[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22) and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"),[[20]](#cite_note-Posada2-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25) with those brought by the [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora"),[[23]](#cite_note-26) as well as with those of [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") civilizations that predate colonization.[[24]](#cite_note-27) [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") is the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"), although [Creole](/wiki/San_Andr%C3%A9s%E2%80%93Providencia_Creole "San Andrés–Providencia Creole"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [64 other languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Colombia "Languages of Colombia") are recognized regionally.

Colombia has been home to many [indigenous peoples and cultures](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Colombia "Indigenous peoples in Colombia") since at least 12,000 BCE. The Spanish landed in [La Guajira](/wiki/Guajira_Peninsula "Guajira Peninsula") in 1499, and by the mid-16th century had colonized much of present-day Colombia, and established the [New Kingdom of Granada](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Granada "New Kingdom of Granada"). [Independence](/wiki/Colombian_War_of_Independence "Colombian War of Independence") from the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") was declared in 1810, with what is now Colombia emerging as the [United Provinces of New Granada](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_New_Granada "United Provinces of New Granada"). After a [Spanish reconquest](/wiki/Spanish_reconquest_of_New_Granada "Spanish reconquest of New Granada"), Colombian independence was secured and the [Gran Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia") period began in 1819. The new polity experimented with federalism as the [Granadine Confederation](/wiki/Granadine_Confederation "Granadine Confederation") (1858) and then the [United States of Colombia](/wiki/United_States_of_Colombia "United States of Colombia") (1863), before becoming a centralised republic in [1886](/wiki/Colombian_Constitution_of_1886 "Colombian Constitution of 1886"). With the backing of the US and France, [Panama seceded](/wiki/Secession_of_Panama_from_Colombia "Secession of Panama from Colombia") from Colombia in 1903, resulting in Colombia's present borders. Beginning in the 1960s, the country has suffered from an asymmetric low-intensity [armed conflict](/wiki/Colombian_conflict "Colombian conflict") and political violence, both of which escalated in the 1990s. Since 2000, there has been improvement in security, stability, and rule of law, as well as economic growth and development.[[25]](#cite_note-Enough_Already!-28)[[26]](#cite_note-Colombia's_GDP_growth-29)

Colombia is recognized for its [healthcare system](/wiki/Health_care_in_Colombia "Health care in Colombia"), being the best healthcare in [The Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") according to the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") and 22nd in the world.[[27]](#cite_note-30)[[28]](#cite_note-31) Its [diversified economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Colombia "Economy of Colombia") is the third-largest in South America, with macroeconomic stability and favorable long-term growth prospects.[[29]](#cite_note-GDP-32)[[30]](#cite_note-strongmacroeconomicmanagement-33) The country is one of the most progressive in the world in [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_by_country_or_territory "LGBTQ rights by country or territory"), [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality"), [animal rights](/wiki/Animal_rights "Animal rights"), and [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"), having legalized [same-sex adoption](/wiki/Same-sex_adoption "Same-sex adoption"), [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), [legality of abortion](/wiki/Abortion_law "Abortion law"), [legality of euthanasia](/wiki/Legality_of_euthanasia "Legality of euthanasia"), and [animal rights protections](/wiki/Animal_rights_movement "Animal rights movement").[[31]](#cite_note-34)[[32]](#cite_note-35)[[33]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39)

Colombia is one of the world's seventeen [megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries"); it has the highest level of [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity_of_Colombia "Biodiversity of Colombia") per square mile in the world, and second-highest level overall.[[37]](#cite_note-Biodiversity_of_Colombia-40) Its territory encompasses the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_natural_region "Amazon natural region"), [highlands](/wiki/Andean_natural_region "Andean natural region"), [grasslands](/wiki/Orinoqu%C3%ADa_natural_region "Orinoquía natural region") and [deserts](/wiki/Tatacoa_Desert "Tatacoa Desert"). Colombia is a member of global and regional organizations including the [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [WTO](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), the [OAS](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States"), the [CIVETS](/wiki/CIVETS "CIVETS"), the [Pacific Alliance](/wiki/Pacific_Alliance "Pacific Alliance") and the [Andean Community](/wiki/Andean_Community "Andean Community"); it is a [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") Global Partner[[38]](#cite_note-41) and a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[39]](#cite_note-:1-42)

## Etymology

The name "Colombia" is derived from the last name of the Italian navigator [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Christophorus Columbus*, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Cristoforo Colombo*, Spanish: *Cristóbal Colón*). It was conceived as a reference to all of the New World.[[40]](#cite_note-43) The name was later adopted by the [Republic of Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia") of 1819, formed from the territories of the old [Viceroyalty of New Granada](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada "Viceroyalty of New Granada") (modern-day Colombia, Panama, Venezuela, Ecuador, and northwest Brazil).[[41]](#cite_note-LABLAA5-44)

When Venezuela, Ecuador, and [Cundinamarca](/wiki/Cundinamarca_Department_(1820) "Cundinamarca Department (1820)") came to exist as independent states, the former [Department of Cundinamarca](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Republic_of_New_Granada "Provinces of the Republic of New Granada") adopted the name "[Republic of New Granada](/wiki/Republic_of_New_Granada "Republic of New Granada")". New Granada officially changed its name in 1858 to the [Granadine Confederation](/wiki/Granadine_Confederation "Granadine Confederation"). In 1863 the name was again changed, this time to [United States of Colombia](/wiki/United_States_of_Colombia "United States of Colombia"), before finally adopting its present name – the Republic of Colombia – in 1886.[[41]](#cite_note-LABLAA5-44)

To refer to this country, the Colombian government uses the terms *Colombia* and *República de Colombia*.[[42]](#cite_note-45)

## History

Main articles: [History of Colombia](/wiki/History_of_Colombia "History of Colombia") and [Timeline of Colombian history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Colombian_history "Timeline of Colombian history")

### Pre-Columbian era

Main article: [Pre-Columbian cultures of Colombia](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_cultures_of_Colombia "Pre-Columbian cultures of Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Culturas_precolombinas_de_Colombia.png/250px-Culturas_precolombinas_de_Colombia.png)](/wiki/File:Culturas_precolombinas_de_Colombia.png)

Location map of the pre-Columbian cultures of Colombia

Owing to its location, the present territory of Colombia was a corridor of early human civilization from [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") to the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") and [Amazon basin](/wiki/Amazon_basin "Amazon basin"). The oldest archaeological finds are from the Pubenza and El Totumo sites in the Magdalena Valley 100 kilometers (62 mi) southwest of Bogotá.[[43]](#cite_note-46) These sites date from the [Paleoindian](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians") period (18,000–8000 BCE). At [Puerto Hormiga](/wiki/Puerto_Hormiga_archaeological_site "Puerto Hormiga archaeological site") and other sites, traces from the [Archaic Period](/wiki/Archaic_Period_(Americas) "Archaic Period (Americas)") (~8000–2000 BCE) have been found. Vestiges indicate that there was also early occupation in the regions of [El Abra](/wiki/El_Abra "El Abra") and [Tequendama](/wiki/Tequendama "Tequendama") in [Cundinamarca](/wiki/Cundinamarca_Department "Cundinamarca Department"). The oldest pottery discovered in the Americas, found in [San Jacinto](/wiki/San_Jacinto,_Bol%C3%ADvar "San Jacinto, Bolívar"), dates to 5000–4000 BCE.[[44]](#cite_note-47)[[45]](#cite_note-48)

Indigenous people inhabited the territory that is now Colombia by 12,500 BCE. Nomadic [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") tribes at the El Abra, [Tibitó](/wiki/Tibit%C3%B3 "Tibitó") and Tequendama sites near present-day [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá") traded with one another and with other cultures from the [Magdalena River](/wiki/Magdalena_River "Magdalena River") Valley.[[46]](#cite_note-49) A site including eight miles (13 km) of [pictographs](/wiki/Pictogram "Pictogram") that is under study at Serranía de la Lindosa was revealed in November 2020.[[47]](#cite_note-50) Their age is suggested as being 12,500 years old (c. 10,480 B.C.) by the anthropologists working on the site, because of extinct fauna depicted. It was during the earliest known human occupation of the area.

Between 5000 and 1000 BCE, hunter-gatherer tribes transitioned to agrarian societies; fixed settlements were established, and pottery appeared. Beginning in the 1st millennium BCE, groups of [Amerindians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") including the [Muisca](/wiki/Muisca "Muisca"), [Zenú](/wiki/Zen%C3%BA "Zenú"), [Quimbaya](/wiki/Quimbaya_civilization "Quimbaya civilization"), and [Tairona](/wiki/Tairona "Tairona") developed the political system of *[cacicazgos](/wiki/Cacicazgo "Cacicazgo")* with a pyramidal structure of power headed by *caciques*. The Muisca inhabited mainly the area of what is now the [Departments](/wiki/Departments_of_Colombia "Departments of Colombia") of [Boyacá](/wiki/Boyac%C3%A1_Department "Boyacá Department") and [Cundinamarca](/wiki/Cundinamarca_Department "Cundinamarca Department") high plateau (*[Altiplano Cundiboyacense](/wiki/Altiplano_Cundiboyacense "Altiplano Cundiboyacense")*) where they formed the [Muisca Confederation](/wiki/Muisca_Confederation "Muisca Confederation"). They farmed maize, potato, quinoa, and cotton, and traded gold, [emeralds](/wiki/Colombian_emeralds "Colombian emeralds"), blankets, ceramic handicrafts, coca and especially [rock salt](/wiki/Halite "Halite") with neighboring nations. The Tairona inhabited northern Colombia in the isolated mountain range of [Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta](/wiki/Sierra_Nevada_de_Santa_Marta "Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta").[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52) The Quimbaya inhabited regions of the [Cauca River](/wiki/Cauca_River "Cauca River") Valley between the [Western](/wiki/Cordillera_Occidental_(Colombia) "Cordillera Occidental (Colombia)") and [Central](/wiki/Cordillera_Central_(Colombia) "Cordillera Central (Colombia)") Ranges of the Colombian Andes.[[50]](#cite_note-53) Most of the Amerindians practiced agriculture and the social structure of each indigenous community was different. Some groups of indigenous people such as the Caribs lived in a state of permanent war, but others had less bellicose attitudes.[[51]](#cite_note-54) During the 1200s, [Malayo-Polynesians](/wiki/Austronesian_expansion "Austronesian expansion") and [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Colombia "Indigenous peoples in Colombia") in Colombia made contact, thereby spreading Native American genetics from Precolonial Colombia to some Pacific Ocean islands.[[52]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56) The coconuts found in Colombia which originally come from the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") originate from this Precolombian exchange.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

### Colonial period

Main articles: [New Kingdom of Granada](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Granada "New Kingdom of Granada") and [Viceroyalty of New Granada](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada "Viceroyalty of New Granada")

See also: [Spanish conquest of New Granada](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_New_Granada "Spanish conquest of New Granada"), [Spanish conquest of the Muisca](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Muisca "Spanish conquest of the Muisca"), [Spanish colonization of the Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas"), and [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Retrato_de_Vasco_Nu%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa_%281475-1517%29_-_An%C3%B3nimo.jpg/250px-Retrato_de_Vasco_Nu%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa_%281475-1517%29_-_An%C3%B3nimo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Retrato_de_Vasco_Nu%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa_(1475-1517)_-_An%C3%B3nimo.jpg)

[Vasco Núñez de Balboa](/wiki/Vasco_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa "Vasco Núñez de Balboa"), founder of [Santa María la Antigua del Darién](/wiki/Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_la_Antigua_del_Dari%C3%A9n "Santa María la Antigua del Darién"), the first stable European settlement on the continent

[Alonso de Ojeda](/wiki/Alonso_de_Ojeda "Alonso de Ojeda") (who had sailed with Columbus) reached the [Guajira Peninsula](/wiki/Guajira_Peninsula "Guajira Peninsula") in 1499.[[55]](#cite_note-LABLAA6-58)[[56]](#cite_note-59) Spanish explorers, led by [Rodrigo de Bastidas](/wiki/Rodrigo_de_Bastidas "Rodrigo de Bastidas"), made the first exploration of the [Caribbean coast](/wiki/Caribbean_Basin "Caribbean Basin") in 1500.[[57]](#cite_note-60) [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") navigated near the Caribbean in 1502.[[58]](#cite_note-61) In 1508, [Vasco Núñez de Balboa](/wiki/Vasco_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa "Vasco Núñez de Balboa") accompanied an expedition to the territory through the region of [Gulf of Urabá](/wiki/Gulf_of_Urab%C3%A1 "Gulf of Urabá") and they founded the town of [Santa María la Antigua del Darién](/wiki/Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_la_Antigua_del_Dari%C3%A9n "Santa María la Antigua del Darién") in 1510, the first stable settlement on the continent.[[d]](#cite_note-62)[[59]](#cite_note-63) [Santa Marta](/wiki/Santa_Marta "Santa Marta") was founded in 1525,[[60]](#cite_note-64) and [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia") in 1533.[[61]](#cite_note-65) Spanish [conquistador](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador") [Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada](/wiki/Gonzalo_Jim%C3%A9nez_de_Quesada "Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada") led an expedition to the interior in April 1536, and christened the districts through which he passed "[New Kingdom of Granada](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Granada "New Kingdom of Granada")". In August 1538, he provisionally founded its capital near the Muisca [cacicazgo](/wiki/Cacicazgo "Cacicazgo") of [Muyquytá](/wiki/Bacat%C3%A1 "Bacatá"), and named it "Santa Fe". The name soon acquired a suffix and was called Santa Fe de Bogotá.[[62]](#cite_note-66)[[63]](#cite_note-JaramilloUribe1989-67) Two other notable journeys by early conquistadors to the interior took place in the same period. [Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar"), conqueror of [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito"), traveled north and founded [Cali](/wiki/Cali "Cali"), in 1536, and [Popayán](/wiki/Popay%C3%A1n "Popayán"), in 1537;[[64]](#cite_note-68) from 1536 to 1539, German conquistador [Nikolaus Federmann](/wiki/Nikolaus_Federmann "Nikolaus Federmann") crossed the [Llanos Orientales](/wiki/Orinoqu%C3%ADa_natural_region "Orinoquía natural region") and went over the [Cordillera Oriental](/wiki/Cordillera_Oriental_(Colombia) "Cordillera Oriental (Colombia)") in a search for [El Dorado](/wiki/El_Dorado "El Dorado"), the "city of gold".[[65]](#cite_note-69)[[66]](#cite_note-70) The legend and the gold would play a pivotal role in luring the Spanish and other Europeans to New Granada during the 16th and 17th centuries.[[67]](#cite_note-71)

The [conquistadors](/wiki/List_of_conquistadors_in_Colombia "List of conquistadors in Colombia") made frequent alliances with the enemies of different indigenous communities. [Indigenous allies](/wiki/Indian_auxiliaries "Indian auxiliaries") were crucial to conquest, as well as to creating and maintaining empire.[[68]](#cite_note-72) Indigenous peoples in Colombia experienced a decline in population due to conquest as well as Eurasian diseases, such as [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), to which they had no immunity.[[69]](#cite_note-73)[[70]](#cite_note-74) Regarding the land as deserted, the Spanish Crown sold properties to all persons interested in colonized territories, creating large farms and possession of mines.[[71]](#cite_note-TierrasColonia-75)[[72]](#cite_note-EconomíaColonial-76)[[73]](#cite_note-políticaeconómica-77) In the 16th century, the [nautical science](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") in Spain reached a great development thanks to numerous scientific figures of the [Casa de Contratación](/wiki/Casa_de_Contrataci%C3%B3n "Casa de Contratación") and nautical science was an essential pillar of the [Iberian expansion](/wiki/Iberian_ship_development,_1400%E2%80%931600 "Iberian ship development, 1400–1600").[[74]](#cite_note-Alonso_de_Santa_Cruz-78) In 1542, the region of New Granada, along with all other Spanish possessions in South America, became part of the [Viceroyalty of Peru](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_Peru "Viceroyalty of Peru"), with its capital in [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima").[[75]](#cite_note-79) In 1547, New Granada became a separate captaincy-general within the viceroyalty, with its capital at Santa Fe de Bogota.[[76]](#cite_note-80) In 1549, the [Royal Audiencia](/wiki/Real_Audiencia "Real Audiencia") was created by a royal decree, and New Granada was ruled by the [Royal Audience of Santa Fe de Bogotá](/wiki/Royal_Audience_of_Santa_Fe_de_Bogot%C3%A1 "Royal Audience of Santa Fe de Bogotá"), which at that time comprised the provinces of Santa Marta, Rio de San Juan, Popayán, Guayana and Cartagena.[[77]](#cite_note-81) But important decisions were taken from the colony to Spain by the [Council of the Indies](/wiki/Council_of_the_Indies "Council of the Indies").[[78]](#cite_note-82)[[79]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Defensa_de_Cartagena_de_Indias_por_la_escuadra_de_D._Blas_de_Lezo%2C_a%C3%B1o_1741.jpg/250px-Defensa_de_Cartagena_de_Indias_por_la_escuadra_de_D._Blas_de_Lezo%2C_a%C3%B1o_1741.jpg)](/wiki/File:Defensa_de_Cartagena_de_Indias_por_la_escuadra_de_D._Blas_de_Lezo,_a%C3%B1o_1741.jpg)

An illustration of the [Battle of Cartagena de Indias](/wiki/Battle_of_Cartagena_de_Indias "Battle of Cartagena de Indias"), a major Spanish victory in the [War of Jenkins' Ear](/wiki/War_of_Jenkins%27_Ear "War of Jenkins' Ear")[[80]](#cite_note-BattleofCartagena-84)

In the 16th century, European slave traders had begun to bring [enslaved Africans](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") to the Americas. Spain was the only European power that did not establish [factories](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") in Africa to purchase slaves; the Spanish Empire instead relied on the [asiento](/wiki/Asiento "Asiento") system, awarding merchants from other European nations the license to trade enslaved peoples to their overseas territories.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-86) This system brought Africans to Colombia, although many spoke out against the institution.[[e]](#cite_note-89)[[f]](#cite_note-92) The indigenous peoples could not be enslaved because they were legally [subjects](/wiki/Commoners "Commoners") of the Spanish Crown.[[87]](#cite_note-93) To protect the indigenous peoples, several forms of land ownership and regulation were established by the Spanish colonial authorities: *resguardos*, *encomiendas* and *haciendas*.[[71]](#cite_note-TierrasColonia-75)[[72]](#cite_note-EconomíaColonial-76)[[73]](#cite_note-políticaeconómica-77)

However, secret anti-Spanish discontentment was already brewing for Colombians since Spain prohibited direct trade between the [Viceroyalty of Peru](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_Peru "Viceroyalty of Peru"), which included Colombia, and the [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain "Viceroyalty of New Spain"), which included the Philippines, the source of Asian products like silk and porcelain which was in demand in the Americas. Illegal trade between Peruvians, Filipinos, and Mexicans continued in secret, as smuggled Asian goods ended up in [Córdoba, Colombia](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba_Department "Córdoba Department"), the distribution center for illegal Asian imports, due to the collusion between these peoples against the authorities in Spain. They settled and traded with each other while disobeying the forced Spanish monopoly.[[88]](#cite_note-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg/250px-Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada_(orthographic_projection).svg)

Map of the [Viceroyalty of New Granada](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada "Viceroyalty of New Granada")

The [Viceroyalty of New Granada](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada "Viceroyalty of New Granada") was established in 1717, then temporarily removed, and then re-established in 1739. Its capital was Santa Fé de Bogotá. This Viceroyalty included some other provinces of northwestern South America that had previously been under the jurisdiction of the [Viceroyalties of New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") or [Peru](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_Peru "Viceroyalty of Peru") and correspond mainly to today's Venezuela, Ecuador, and Panama. Bogotá became one of the principal administrative centers of the Spanish possessions in the New World, along with [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima") and [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), though it remained less developed compared to those two cities in several economic and logistical ways.[[89]](#cite_note-95)[[90]](#cite_note-96)

[Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") declared war [on Spain](/wiki/War_of_Jenkins%27_Ear "War of Jenkins' Ear") in 1739, and the city of Cartagena quickly became a top target for the British. A massive British expeditionary force was dispatched to capture the city, but, after achieving initial inroads, devastating outbreaks of disease crippled their numbers, and the British were forced to withdraw. The battle became one of Spain's most decisive victories in the conflict, and secured Spanish dominance in the Caribbean until the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War").[[80]](#cite_note-BattleofCartagena-84)[[91]](#cite_note-97) The 18th-century priest, botanist, and mathematician [José Celestino Mutis](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Celestino_Mutis "José Celestino Mutis") was delegated by Viceroy [Antonio Caballero y Góngora](/wiki/Antonio_Caballero_y_G%C3%B3ngora "Antonio Caballero y Góngora") to conduct an inventory of the nature of New Granada. Started in 1783, this became known as the [Royal Botanical Expedition to New Granada](/wiki/Royal_Botanical_Expedition_to_New_Granada "Royal Botanical Expedition to New Granada"). It classified plants and wildlife, and founded the first astronomical observatory in the city of Santa Fe de Bogotá.[[92]](#cite_note-98) In July 1801 the Prussian scientist [Alexander von Humboldt](/wiki/Alexander_von_Humboldt "Alexander von Humboldt") reached Santa Fe de Bogotá where he met with Mutis. In addition, historical figures in the process of independence in New Granada emerged from the expedition as the astronomer [Francisco José de Caldas](/wiki/Francisco_Jos%C3%A9_de_Caldas "Francisco José de Caldas"), the scientist [Francisco Antonio Zea](/wiki/Francisco_Antonio_Zea "Francisco Antonio Zea"), the zoologist [Jorge Tadeo Lozano](/wiki/Jorge_Tadeo_Lozano "Jorge Tadeo Lozano") and the painter [Salvador Rizo](/wiki/Salvador_Rizo "Salvador Rizo").[[93]](#cite_note-99)[[94]](#cite_note-100)

### Independence

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | **The [accessibility](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Accessibility "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility") of this section is in question**. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Colombia "Talk:Colombia"). *(November 2024)* |

Main articles: [Colombian War of Independence](/wiki/Colombian_War_of_Independence "Colombian War of Independence"), [First Republic of New Granada](/wiki/First_Republic_of_New_Granada "First Republic of New Granada"), and [Gran Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/AGHRC_%281890%29_-_Carta_XI_-_Divisi%C3%B3n_pol%C3%ADtica_de_Colombia%2C_1824.jpg/330px-AGHRC_%281890%29_-_Carta_XI_-_Divisi%C3%B3n_pol%C3%ADtica_de_Colombia%2C_1824.jpg)](/wiki/File:AGHRC_(1890)_-_Carta_XI_-_Divisi%C3%B3n_pol%C3%ADtica_de_Colombia,_1824.jpg)

The departments of Gran Colombia in 1824

Rebellions against Spanish rule had occurred in the empire since the advent of conquest and colonization, but most were either crushed or remained too weak to change the overall situation. The last one that sought outright independence from Spain sprang up around 1810 and culminated in the Colombian Declaration of Independence, issued on 20 July 1810, the day that is now celebrated as the nation's Independence Day.[[95]](#cite_note-101) This movement followed the independence of [Saint-Domingue](/wiki/Saint-Domingue "Saint-Domingue") (present-day Haiti) in 1804, which provided some support to an eventual leader of this rebellion: [Simón Bolívar](/wiki/Sim%C3%B3n_Bol%C3%ADvar "Simón Bolívar"). [Francisco de Paula Santander](/wiki/Francisco_de_Paula_Santander "Francisco de Paula Santander") also would play a decisive role.[[96]](#cite_note-102)[[97]](#cite_note-103)[[98]](#cite_note-104)

A movement was initiated by [Antonio Nariño](/wiki/Antonio_Nari%C3%B1o "Antonio Nariño"), who opposed Spanish centralism and led the opposition against the [Viceroyalty](/wiki/Viceroyalty "Viceroyalty").[[99]](#cite_note-105) [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia") became independent in November 1811.[[100]](#cite_note-106) In 1811, the [United Provinces of New Granada](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_New_Granada "United Provinces of New Granada") were proclaimed, headed by [Camilo Torres Tenorio](/wiki/Camilo_Torres_Tenorio "Camilo Torres Tenorio").[[101]](#cite_note-107)[[102]](#cite_note-108) The emergence of two distinct ideological currents among the patriots ([federalism](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") and [centralism](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state")) gave rise to a period of instability called the [Patria Boba](/wiki/Patria_Boba "Patria Boba").[[103]](#cite_note-109) Shortly after the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") ended, [Ferdinand VII](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII "Ferdinand VII"), recently restored to the throne in Spain, unexpectedly decided [to send military forces](/wiki/Spanish_reconquest_of_New_Granada "Spanish reconquest of New Granada") to retake most of northern South America. The viceroyalty was restored under the command of [Juan de Sámano](/wiki/Juan_de_S%C3%A1mano "Juan de Sámano"), whose regime punished those who participated in the patriotic movements, ignoring the political nuances of the [juntas](/wiki/Junta_(Spanish_American_Independence) "Junta (Spanish American Independence)").[[104]](#cite_note-110) The retribution stoked renewed rebellion, which, combined with a weakened Spain, made possible a successful rebellion led by the Venezuelan-born [Simón Bolívar](/wiki/Military_career_of_Sim%C3%B3n_Bol%C3%ADvar "Military career of Simón Bolívar"), who finally proclaimed [independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence") in 1819.[[105]](#cite_note-Historia_ilustrada_de_Colombia-111)[[106]](#cite_note-112) The [pro-Spanish resistance](/wiki/Royalist_(Spanish_American_Independence) "Royalist (Spanish American Independence)") was defeated in 1822 in the present territory of Colombia and in 1823 in Venezuela.[[107]](#cite_note-113)[[108]](#cite_note-114)[[109]](#cite_note-115) During the Independence War, between 250 and 400 thousand people died (12–20% of the pre-war population).[[110]](#cite_note-19thcentury-116)[[111]](#cite_note-Deremilitari-117)[[112]](#cite_note-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Cambios_territoriales_de_Colombia.gif/330px-Cambios_territoriales_de_Colombia.gif)](/wiki/File:Cambios_territoriales_de_Colombia.gif)

Changes to the borders of Colombia between 1811 and 2012

The territory of the Viceroyalty of New Granada became the [Republic of Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia"), organized as a [union of the current territories](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Gran_Colombia "Subdivisions of Gran Colombia") of Colombia, Panama, Ecuador, Venezuela, parts of Guyana and Brazil and north of [Marañón River](/wiki/Mara%C3%B1%C3%B3n_River "Marañón River").[[113]](#cite_note-119) The [Congress of Cúcuta](/wiki/Congress_of_C%C3%BAcuta "Congress of Cúcuta") in 1821 adopted a [constitution](/wiki/Colombian_Constitution_of_1821 "Colombian Constitution of 1821") for the new Republic.[[114]](#cite_note-120)[[115]](#cite_note-121) Simón Bolívar became the first [President of Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia"), and Francisco de Paula Santander was made [Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Colombia "Vice President of Colombia").[[116]](#cite_note-122) However, the new republic was unstable and the Gran Colombia ultimately collapsed.

Modern Colombia comes from one of the countries that emerged after the [dissolution of Gran Colombia](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Gran_Colombia "Dissolution of Gran Colombia"), the other two being Ecuador and Venezuela.[[117]](#cite_note-EtHisColombia-123)[[118]](#cite_note-GranColombiaNuevaGranada-124)[[119]](#cite_note-125) Colombia was the first [constitutional government](/wiki/Constitutional_government "Constitutional government") in South America,[[120]](#cite_note-HistoriaConstitucional-126) and the [Liberal](/wiki/Colombian_Liberal_Party "Colombian Liberal Party") and [Conservative](/wiki/Colombian_Conservative_Party "Colombian Conservative Party") parties, founded in 1848 and 1849, respectively, are two of the oldest surviving political parties in the Americas.[[121]](#cite_note-127) [Slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") was abolished in the country in 1851.[[122]](#cite_note-128)[[123]](#cite_note-129)

Internal political and territorial divisions led to the dissolution of [Gran Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia") in 1830.[[117]](#cite_note-EtHisColombia-123)[[118]](#cite_note-GranColombiaNuevaGranada-124) The so-called "[Department of Cundinamarca](/wiki/Cundinamarca_Province "Cundinamarca Province")" adopted the name "[New Granada](/wiki/Republic_of_the_New_Granada "Republic of the New Granada")", which it kept until 1858 when it became the "Confederación Granadina" ([Granadine Confederation](/wiki/Granadine_Confederation "Granadine Confederation")). After a [two-year civil war](/wiki/Colombian_Civil_War_(1860%E2%80%931862) "Colombian Civil War (1860–1862)") in 1863, the [United States of Colombia](/wiki/United_States_of_Colombia "United States of Colombia") was created, which became known as the Republic of Colombia in 1886.[[120]](#cite_note-HistoriaConstitucional-126)[[124]](#cite_note-130) Internal divisions remained between the bipartisan political forces, occasionally igniting very bloody civil wars, the most significant being the [Thousand Days' War](/wiki/Thousand_Days%27_War "Thousand Days' War") (1899–1902), in which between 100 and 180 thousand Colombians lost their lives when the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Colombian_Liberal_Party "Colombian Liberal Party"), supported by [Venezuela](/wiki/United_States_of_Venezuela "United States of Venezuela"), [Ecuador](/wiki/History_of_Ecuador_(1895%E2%80%931925) "History of Ecuador (1895–1925)"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") rebelled against the [Nationalist government](/wiki/Colombian_Conservative_Party "Colombian Conservative Party") and took control of [Santander](/wiki/Santander_State "Santander State"), ultimately being defeated in 1902 by nationalist forces.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

### 20th century

See also: [Colombian conflict](/wiki/Colombian_conflict "Colombian conflict") and [La Violencia](/wiki/La_Violencia "La Violencia")

The United States' efforts to assert influence over the region, particularly regarding the construction and control of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"),[[126]](#cite_note-132) contributed to the [secession of the Department of Panama](/wiki/Secession_of_Panama_from_Colombia "Secession of Panama from Colombia") in 1903 and its political independence.[[127]](#cite_note-SeparationofPanama-133) The United States paid Colombia $25,000,000 in 1921, seven years after completion of the canal, as redress for President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt")'s role in the creation of Panama, and Colombia recognized Panama under the terms of the [Thomson–Urrutia Treaty](/wiki/Thomson%E2%80%93Urrutia_Treaty "Thomson–Urrutia Treaty").[[128]](#cite_note-134) Colombia and Peru went to [war](/wiki/Leticia_Incident "Leticia Incident") because of territory disputes far in the [Amazon basin](/wiki/Amazon_basin "Amazon basin"). The war ended with a peace deal brokered by the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"). The League finally awarded the disputed area to Colombia in June 1934.[[129]](#cite_note-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Bogotazo.jpg/250px-Bogotazo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bogotazo.jpg)

The [Bogotazo](/wiki/Bogotazo "Bogotazo") in 1948

Soon after, Colombia achieved some degree of political stability, which was interrupted by a bloody conflict that took place between the late 1940s and the early 1950s, a period known as *[La Violencia](/wiki/La_Violencia "La Violencia")* ("The Violence"). Its cause was mainly mounting tensions between the two leading political parties, which subsequently ignited after the assassination of the Liberal presidential candidate [Jorge Eliécer Gaitán](/wiki/Jorge_Eli%C3%A9cer_Gait%C3%A1n "Jorge Eliécer Gaitán") on 9 April 1948.[[130]](#cite_note-136)[[131]](#cite_note-137) The ensuing riots in Bogotá, known as [El Bogotazo](/wiki/El_Bogotazo "El Bogotazo"), spread throughout the country and claimed the lives of at least 180,000 Colombians.[[132]](#cite_note-Encarta-138)

Colombia entered the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") when [Laureano Gómez](/wiki/Laureano_G%C3%B3mez "Laureano Gómez") was elected president. It was the only Latin American country to join the war in a direct military role as an ally of the United States. Particularly important was the resistance of the Colombian troops at [Old Baldy](/wiki/Battle_of_Old_Baldy "Battle of Old Baldy").[[133]](#cite_note-Colombia's_legacy_with_Korea-139)

The violence between the two political parties decreased first when [Gustavo Rojas](/wiki/Gustavo_Rojas_Pinilla "Gustavo Rojas Pinilla") deposed the [President](/wiki/Laureano_G%C3%B3mez "Laureano Gómez") of Colombia in a coup d'état and negotiated with the guerrillas, and then under the [military junta](/wiki/Colombian_Military_Junta "Colombian Military Junta") of General [Gabriel París](/wiki/Gabriel_Par%C3%ADs "Gabriel París").[[134]](#cite_note-140)[[135]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Centro_de_Memoria_Hist%C3%B3rica_-_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg/250px-Centro_de_Memoria_Hist%C3%B3rica_-_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Centro_de_Memoria_Hist%C3%B3rica_-_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg)

The Axis of Peace and Memory, a memorial to the victims of the [Colombian conflict](/wiki/Colombian_conflict "Colombian conflict") (1964–present)

After Rojas' deposition, the Colombian Conservative Party and the Colombian Liberal Party agreed to create the [National Front](/wiki/National_Front_(Colombia) "National Front (Colombia)"), a coalition that would jointly govern the country. Under the deal, the presidency would alternate between conservatives and liberals every 4 years for 16 years; the two parties would have parity in all other elective offices.[[136]](#cite_note-142) The National Front ended "La Violencia", and National Front administrations attempted to institute far-reaching social and economic reforms in cooperation with the [Alliance for Progress](/wiki/Alliance_for_Progress "Alliance for Progress").[[137]](#cite_note-143)[[138]](#cite_note-144) Despite the progress in certain sectors, many social and political problems continued, and guerrilla groups were formally created such as the [FARC](/wiki/Revolutionary_Armed_Forces_of_Colombia "Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia"), the [ELN](/wiki/National_Liberation_Army_(Colombia) "National Liberation Army (Colombia)") and the [M-19](/wiki/19th_of_April_Movement "19th of April Movement") to fight the government and political apparatus.[[139]](#cite_note-145)

Since the 1960s, the country has suffered from an [asymmetric](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare") [low-intensity](/wiki/Low_intensity_conflict "Low intensity conflict") [armed conflict](/wiki/Colombian_armed_conflict_(1964%E2%80%93present) "Colombian armed conflict (1964–present)") between [government forces](/wiki/Military_Forces_of_Colombia "Military Forces of Colombia"), [leftist guerrilla groups](/wiki/Guerrilla_movements_in_Colombia "Guerrilla movements in Colombia") and [right wing paramilitaries](/wiki/Paramilitarism_in_Colombia "Paramilitarism in Colombia").[[140]](#cite_note-HistoricalCommission-146) The conflict escalated in the 1990s,[[141]](#cite_note-Colombian_armed_conflict-147) mainly in remote rural areas.[[142]](#cite_note-Colombian_conflict-148) Since the beginning of the armed conflict, [human rights defenders](/wiki/Human_rights_defenders "Human rights defenders") have fought for the respect for human rights, despite staggering opposition.[[g]](#cite_note-151)[[h]](#cite_note-153) Several guerrillas' organizations decided to demobilize after peace negotiations in 1989–1994.[[25]](#cite_note-Enough_Already!-28)

The United States has been heavily involved in the conflict since its beginnings, when in the early 1960s the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") encouraged the Colombian military to attack leftist militias in rural Colombia. This was part of the U.S. fight against communism. [Mercenaries](/wiki/Mercenarie "Mercenarie") and multinational corporations such as [Chiquita Brands International](/wiki/Chiquita_Brands_International "Chiquita Brands International") are some of the international actors that have contributed to the violence of the conflict.[[140]](#cite_note-HistoricalCommission-146)[[25]](#cite_note-Enough_Already!-28)[[146]](#cite_note-colombia-and-us-54-154)

Beginning in the mid-1970s Colombian [drug cartels](/wiki/Drug_cartel "Drug cartel") became major producers, processors and exporters of [illegal drugs](/wiki/Illegal_drug_trade_in_Colombia "Illegal drug trade in Colombia"), primarily [marijuana](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") and [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine").[[147]](#cite_note-155)

On 4 July 1991, a new [Constitution](/wiki/Colombian_Constitution_of_1991 "Colombian Constitution of 1991") was promulgated. The changes generated by the new constitution are viewed as positive by Colombian society.[[148]](#cite_note-156)[[149]](#cite_note-Colombian_Constitution_of_1991-157)

### 21st century

See also: [Colombian peace process](/wiki/Colombian_peace_process "Colombian peace process")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Jefa_de_Estado_participa_en_ceremonia_de_la_Firma_de_la_Paz_entre_el_Gobierno_de_Colombia_y_las_FARC_E.P._%2829953487045%29.jpg/250px-Jefa_de_Estado_participa_en_ceremonia_de_la_Firma_de_la_Paz_entre_el_Gobierno_de_Colombia_y_las_FARC_E.P._%2829953487045%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jefa_de_Estado_participa_en_ceremonia_de_la_Firma_de_la_Paz_entre_el_Gobierno_de_Colombia_y_las_FARC_E.P._(29953487045).jpg)

Former President [Juan Manuel Santos](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Santos "Juan Manuel Santos") signing a [peace accord](/wiki/Colombian_peace_process "Colombian peace process")

The administration of President [Álvaro Uribe](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Uribe "Álvaro Uribe") (2002–2010) adopted the [democratic security policy](/wiki/Democratic_security_policy "Democratic security policy") which included an integrated [counter-terrorism](/wiki/Counter-terrorism "Counter-terrorism") and [counter-insurgency](/wiki/Counter-insurgency "Counter-insurgency") campaign.[[150]](#cite_note-158) The government economic plan also promoted confidence in investors.[[151]](#cite_note-159) As part of a controversial peace process, the [AUC](/wiki/United_Self-Defense_Forces_of_Colombia "United Self-Defense Forces of Colombia") (right-wing paramilitaries) had ceased to function formally as an organization .[[152]](#cite_note-CIAWFB-160) In February 2008, millions of Colombians demonstrated against FARC and other outlawed groups.[[153]](#cite_note-161)

After peace negotiations in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), the [Colombian government](/wiki/Government_of_Colombia "Government of Colombia") of President [Juan Manuel Santos](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Santos "Juan Manuel Santos") and the [guerrillas](/wiki/Guerrillas "Guerrillas") of the FARC-EP announced a final agreement to end the conflict.[[154]](#cite_note-162) However, a [referendum](/wiki/Colombian_peace_agreement_referendum,_2016 "Colombian peace agreement referendum, 2016") to ratify the deal was unsuccessful.[[155]](#cite_note-163)[[156]](#cite_note-164) Afterward, the Colombian government and the FARC signed a revised [peace deal](/wiki/Colombian_peace_process "Colombian peace process") in November 2016,[[157]](#cite_note-165) which the [Colombian congress](/wiki/Colombian_congress "Colombian congress") approved.[[158]](#cite_note-166) In 2016, President Santos was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/2016_Nobel_Peace_Prize "2016 Nobel Peace Prize").[[159]](#cite_note-167) The Government began a process of attention and comprehensive reparation for victims of conflict.[[160]](#cite_note-168)[[161]](#cite_note-169) Colombia shows modest progress in the struggle to defend human rights, as expressed by [HRW](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch").[[162]](#cite_note-170) A [Special Jurisdiction of Peace](/wiki/Special_Jurisdiction_of_Peace "Special Jurisdiction of Peace") has been created to investigate, clarify, prosecute and punish serious human rights violations and grave breaches of [international humanitarian law](/wiki/International_humanitarian_law "International humanitarian law") which occurred during the armed conflict and to satisfy victims' right to [justice](/wiki/Justice "Justice").[[163]](#cite_note-171) During his visit to Colombia, [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") paid tribute to the victims of the conflict.[[164]](#cite_note-172)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Inauguration_Petro.jpg/250px-Inauguration_Petro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inauguration_Petro.jpg)

[Gustavo Petro](/wiki/Gustavo_Petro "Gustavo Petro"), the country's first [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") president

In June 2018, [Iván Duque](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Duque "Iván Duque"), the candidate of the right-wing [Democratic Center](/wiki/Democratic_Center_(Colombia) "Democratic Center (Colombia)") party, won the presidential [election](/wiki/2018_Colombian_presidential_election "2018 Colombian presidential election").[[165]](#cite_note-173) On 7 August 2018, he was sworn in as the new [President of Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia") to succeed Juan Manuel Santos.[[166]](#cite_note-174) [Colombia's relations with Venezuela](/wiki/2010_Colombia%E2%80%93Venezuela_diplomatic_crisis "2010 Colombia–Venezuela diplomatic crisis") have fluctuated due to ideological differences between the two governments.[[167]](#cite_note-175) Colombia has offered [humanitarian support](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid") with food and medicines to mitigate the shortage of supplies in Venezuela.[[168]](#cite_note-176) Colombia's Foreign Ministry said that all efforts to resolve [Venezuela's crisis](/wiki/Crisis_in_Venezuela_(2012%E2%80%93present) "Crisis in Venezuela (2012–present)") should be peaceful.[[169]](#cite_note-177) Colombia proposed the idea of the [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") and a final document was adopted by the United Nations.[[170]](#cite_note-178) In February 2019, Venezuelan president [Nicolás Maduro](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro "Nicolás Maduro") cut off diplomatic relations with Colombia after Colombian President Ivan Duque had helped Venezuelan opposition politicians deliver humanitarian aid to their country. Colombia recognized Venezuelan opposition leader [Juan Guaidó](/wiki/Juan_Guaid%C3%B3 "Juan Guaidó") as the country's legitimate president. In January 2020, Colombia rejected Maduro's proposal that the two countries restore diplomatic relations.[[171]](#cite_note-179)

[Protests](/wiki/2021_Colombian_protests "2021 Colombian protests") started on 28 April 2021 when the government proposed a tax bill that would greatly expand the range of the 19 percent [value-added tax](/wiki/Value-added_tax "Value-added tax").[[172]](#cite_note-nyb220721-180) The 19 June 2022 [election](/wiki/2022_Colombian_presidential_election "2022 Colombian presidential election") run-off vote ended in a win for former guerrilla, [Gustavo Petro](/wiki/Gustavo_Petro "Gustavo Petro"), taking 50.47% of the vote compared to 47.27% for independent candidate [Rodolfo Hernández](/wiki/Rodolfo_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Su%C3%A1rez "Rodolfo Hernández Suárez"). The single-term limit for the country's presidency prevented President Iván Duque from seeking re-election. On 7 August 2022, Petro was sworn in, becoming the country's first leftist president.[[173]](#cite_note-181)[[174]](#cite_note-182)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Colombia](/wiki/Geography_of_Colombia "Geography of Colombia")

See also: [Geology of Colombia](/wiki/Geology_of_Colombia "Geology of Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Mapa_de_Colombia_%28topograf%C3%ADa%29.svg/250px-Mapa_de_Colombia_%28topograf%C3%ADa%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mapa_de_Colombia_(topograf%C3%ADa).svg)

[Topographic map](/wiki/Topographic_map "Topographic map") of Colombia

The geography of Colombia is characterized by its six main [natural regions](/wiki/Natural_regions_of_Colombia "Natural regions of Colombia") that present their unique characteristics, from the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") mountain range region; the [Pacific Coastal](/wiki/Pacific_Coast "Pacific Coast") region; the Caribbean coastal region; the *[Llanos](/wiki/Llanos "Llanos")* (plains); the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") region; to the [insular area](/wiki/Insular_Region_(Colombia) "Insular Region (Colombia)"), comprising islands in both the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.[[175]](#cite_note-Geography_of_Colombia-183) It shares its maritime limits with [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), and the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic").[[176]](#cite_note-Maritime_borders-184)

Colombia is bordered to the northwest by Panama, to the east by Venezuela and Brazil, and to the south by Ecuador and Peru;[[177]](#cite_note-Land_borders-185) it established its maritime boundaries with neighboring countries through seven agreements on the Caribbean Sea and three on the Pacific Ocean.[[176]](#cite_note-Maritime_borders-184) It lies between latitudes [12°N](/wiki/12th_parallel_north "12th parallel north") and [4°S](/wiki/4th_parallel_south "4th parallel south") and between longitudes [67°](/wiki/67th_meridian_west "67th meridian west") and [79°W](/wiki/79th_meridian_west "79th meridian west").

East of the Andes lies the [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") of the *[Llanos](/wiki/Llanos "Llanos")*, part of the [Orinoco](/wiki/Orinoco "Orinoco") River basin, and in the far southeast, the [jungle](/wiki/Jungle "Jungle") of the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest"). Together these lowlands make up over half Colombia's territory, but they contain less than 6% of the population. To the north the [Caribbean coast](/wiki/Caribbean_Region_of_Colombia "Caribbean Region of Colombia"), home to 21.9% of the population and the location of the major port cities of [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla") and [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia"), generally consists of low-lying plains, but it also contains the [Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta](/wiki/Sierra_Nevada_de_Santa_Marta "Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta") mountain range, which includes the country's tallest peaks ([Pico Cristóbal Colón](/wiki/Pico_Crist%C3%B3bal_Col%C3%B3n "Pico Cristóbal Colón") and [Pico Simón Bolívar](/wiki/Pico_Sim%C3%B3n_Bol%C3%ADvar "Pico Simón Bolívar")), and the [La Guajira Desert](/wiki/La_Guajira_Desert "La Guajira Desert"). By contrast the narrow and discontinuous [Pacific coastal lowlands](/wiki/Pacific_Region_of_Colombia "Pacific Region of Colombia"), backed by the [Serranía de Baudó](/wiki/Baud%C3%B3_Mountains "Baudó Mountains") mountains, are sparsely populated and covered in dense vegetation. The principal Pacific port is [Buenaventura](/wiki/Buenaventura,_Colombia "Buenaventura, Colombia").[[175]](#cite_note-Geography_of_Colombia-183)[[178]](#cite_note-populationbyregions-186)[[179]](#cite_note-Population_density-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/ISS-42_Colombia%E2%80%99s_Santa_Marta_massif.jpg/250px-ISS-42_Colombia%E2%80%99s_Santa_Marta_massif.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISS-42_Colombia%E2%80%99s_Santa_Marta_massif.jpg)

[Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta](/wiki/Sierra_Nevada_de_Santa_Marta "Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta") as seen from the [ISS](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station")

Part of the [Ring of Fire](/wiki/Pacific_Ring_of_Fire "Pacific Ring of Fire"), a region of the world subject to earthquakes and [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"),[[180]](#cite_note-Ring_of_Fire-188) in the interior of Colombia the Andes are the prevailing geographical feature. Most of Colombia's population centers are located in these interior highlands. Beyond the [Colombian Massif](/wiki/Colombian_Massif "Colombian Massif") (in the southwestern departments of [Cauca](/wiki/Cauca_Department "Cauca Department") and [Nariño](/wiki/Nari%C3%B1o_Department "Nariño Department")), these are divided into three branches known as *cordilleras* (mountain ranges): the [Cordillera Occidental](/wiki/Cordillera_Occidental_(Colombia) "Cordillera Occidental (Colombia)"), running adjacent to the Pacific coast and including the city of [Cali](/wiki/Santiago_de_Cali "Santiago de Cali"); the [Cordillera Central](/wiki/Cordillera_Central_(Colombia) "Cordillera Central (Colombia)"), running between the [Cauca](/wiki/Cauca_River "Cauca River") and [Magdalena River](/wiki/Magdalena_River "Magdalena River") valleys (to the west and east, respectively) and including the cities of [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín"), [Manizales](/wiki/Manizales "Manizales"), [Pereira](/wiki/Pereira,_Colombia "Pereira, Colombia"), and [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia,_Quind%C3%ADo "Armenia, Quindío"); and the [Cordillera Oriental](/wiki/Cordillera_Oriental_(Colombia) "Cordillera Oriental (Colombia)"), extending northeast to the [Guajira Peninsula](/wiki/Guajira_Peninsula "Guajira Peninsula") and including Bogotá, [Bucaramanga](/wiki/Bucaramanga "Bucaramanga"), and [Cúcuta](/wiki/C%C3%BAcuta "Cúcuta").[[175]](#cite_note-Geography_of_Colombia-183)[[178]](#cite_note-populationbyregions-186)[[179]](#cite_note-Population_density-187) Peaks in the Cordillera Occidental exceed 4,700 m (15,420 ft), and in the Cordillera Central and Cordillera Oriental they reach 5,000 m (16,404 ft). At 2,600 m (8,530 ft), Bogotá is the highest city of its size in the world.[[175]](#cite_note-Geography_of_Colombia-183)

The main rivers of Colombia are Magdalena, Cauca, [Guaviare](/wiki/Guaviare_River "Guaviare River"), [Atrato](/wiki/Atrato_River "Atrato River"), [Meta](/wiki/Meta_River "Meta River"), [Putumayo](/wiki/Putumayo_River "Putumayo River") and [Caquetá](/wiki/Caquet%C3%A1_River "Caquetá River"). Colombia has four main drainage systems: the Pacific drain, the Caribbean drain, the Orinoco Basin and the Amazon Basin. The Orinoco and [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_River "Amazon River") Rivers mark limits with Colombia to Venezuela and Peru respectively.[[181]](#cite_note-Hydrography-189)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Colombia](/wiki/Climate_of_Colombia "Climate of Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Colombia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Colombia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Colombia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

Colombia map of [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")

The climate of Colombia is characterized for being tropical presenting variations within [six natural regions](/wiki/Natural_regions_of_Colombia "Natural regions of Colombia") and depending on the altitude, temperature, [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity"), winds and rainfall.[[182]](#cite_note-Thermal_floors-190) Colombia has a diverse range of climate zones, including [tropical rainforests](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest"), [savannas](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna"), [steppes](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe"), [deserts](/wiki/Desert "Desert") and mountain climates.

[Mountain climate](/wiki/Mountain_climate "Mountain climate") is one of the unique features of the Andes and other high altitude reliefs where climate is determined by elevation. Below 1,000 meters (3,281 ft) in elevation is the [warm altitudinal zone](/wiki/Tierra_caliente "Tierra caliente"), where temperatures are above 24 °C (75.2 °F). About 82.5% of the country's total area lies in the warm altitudinal zone. The [temperate climate altitudinal zone](/wiki/Tierra_templada "Tierra templada") located between 1,001 and 2,000 meters (3,284 and 6,562 ft) is characterized for presenting an average temperature ranging between 17 and 24 °C (62.6 and 75.2 °F). The [cold climate](/wiki/Tierra_fr%C3%ADa "Tierra fría") is present between 2,001 and 3,000 meters (6,565 and 9,843 ft) and the temperatures vary between 12 and 17 °C (53.6 and 62.6 °F). Beyond lies the alpine conditions of the forested zone and then the treeless grasslands of the [páramos](/wiki/P%C3%A1ramo "Páramo"). Above 4,000 meters (13,123 ft), where temperatures are below freezing, the climate is [glacial](/wiki/Tierra_helada "Tierra helada"), a zone of permanent snow and ice.[[182]](#cite_note-Thermal_floors-190)

### Biodiversity and conservation

See also: [Fauna of Colombia](/wiki/Fauna_of_Colombia "Fauna of Colombia"), [Flora of Colombia](/wiki/Flora_of_Colombia "Flora of Colombia"), and [Deforestation in Colombia](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Colombia "Deforestation in Colombia")

Colombia is one of the [megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries") in [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"),[[183]](#cite_note-191) ranking first in [bird species](/wiki/List_of_birds_of_Colombia "List of birds of Colombia").[[184]](#cite_note-Bird_Species-192) Colombia is the country with the planet's highest biodiversity, having the highest rate of species by area as well as the largest number of [endemisms](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") (species that are not found naturally anywhere else) of any country. About 10% of the species of the Earth live in Colombia, including over 1,900 species of bird, more than in Europe and North America combined. Colombia has 10% of the world's [mammals](/wiki/List_of_mammals_of_Colombia "List of mammals of Colombia") species, 14% of the [amphibian](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian") species and 18% of the [bird](/wiki/Endemic_birds_of_Colombia "Endemic birds of Colombia") species of the world.[[185]](#cite_note-DIVERSITY_OF_SPECIES_IN_COLOMBIA-193)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Cattleya_trianae_tipo_Baronessa.jpg/250px-Cattleya_trianae_tipo_Baronessa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cattleya_trianae_tipo_Baronessa.jpg)

The national flower of Colombia, the [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") [orchid](/wiki/Orchid "Orchid") *[Cattleya trianae](/wiki/Cattleya_trianae "Cattleya trianae")*, is named for Colombian botanist and physician [José Jerónimo Triana](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Jer%C3%B3nimo_Triana "José Jerónimo Triana").[[186]](#cite_note-194)

As for plants, the country has between 40,000 and 45,000 plant [species](/wiki/Species "Species"), equivalent to 10 or 20% of total global species, which is even more remarkable given that Colombia is considered a country of intermediate size.[[187]](#cite_note-195) Colombia is the second most biodiverse country in the world, lagging only after Brazil which is approximately 7 times bigger.[[37]](#cite_note-Biodiversity_of_Colombia-40)

Colombia has about 2,000 species of [marine fish](/wiki/Marine_fish "Marine fish") and is the second most diverse country in [freshwater fish](/wiki/Freshwater_fish "Freshwater fish"). It is also the country with the most endemic species of [butterflies](/wiki/List_of_butterflies_of_Colombia "List of butterflies of Colombia"), is first in [orchid](/wiki/Orchid "Orchid") species, and has approximately 7,000 species of [beetles](/wiki/Beetle "Beetle"). Colombia is second in the number of amphibian species and is the third most diverse country in [reptiles](/wiki/List_of_reptiles_of_Colombia "List of reptiles of Colombia") and [palms](/wiki/Arecaceae "Arecaceae"). There are about 1,900 species of [mollusks](/wiki/Mollusk "Mollusk") and according to estimates there are about 300,000 species of [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrates "Invertebrates") in the country. In Colombia there are 32 terrestrial [biomes](/wiki/Biomes "Biomes") and 314 types of [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystems "Ecosystems").[[188]](#cite_note-196)[[189]](#cite_note-humboldt-197)

Protected areas and the "National Park System" cover an area of about 14,268,224 hectares (142,682.24 km2) and account for 12.77% of the Colombian territory.[[190]](#cite_note-198) Compared to neighboring countries, rates of [deforestation in Colombia](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Colombia "Deforestation in Colombia") are still relatively low.[[191]](#cite_note-United_Nations_Deforestation-199) Colombia had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 8.26/10, ranking it 25th globally out of 172 countries.[[192]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-200) Colombia is the sixth country in the world by magnitude of total renewable freshwater supply, and still has large reserves of freshwater.[[193]](#cite_note-worldwater-201)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Government of Colombia](/wiki/Government_of_Colombia "Government of Colombia")

See also: [Elections in Colombia](/wiki/Elections_in_Colombia "Elections in Colombia") and [Colombian Constitution of 1991](/wiki/Colombian_Constitution_of_1991 "Colombian Constitution of 1991")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Presidente_Gustavo_Petro_Urrego_%283x4_close_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Presidente_Gustavo_Petro_Urrego_%283x4_close_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presidente_Gustavo_Petro_Urrego_(3x4_close_cropped).jpg)

[Gustavo Petro](/wiki/Gustavo_Petro "Gustavo Petro")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/M%C3%A1rquez_en_su_despacho_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-M%C3%A1rquez_en_su_despacho_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%A1rquez_en_su_despacho_(cropped).jpg)

[Francia Márquez](/wiki/Francia_M%C3%A1rquez "Francia Márquez")  
[Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Colombia "Vice President of Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Casa_de_Nari%C3%B1o_y_Vigilantes.jpg/250px-Casa_de_Nari%C3%B1o_y_Vigilantes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_Nari%C3%B1o_y_Vigilantes.jpg)

[Casa de Nariño](/wiki/Casa_de_Nari%C3%B1o "Casa de Nariño") is the official home and principal workplace of the [President of Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia").

The government of Colombia takes place within the framework of a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [participatory democratic](/wiki/Participatory_democracy "Participatory democracy") republic as established in the Constitution of 1991.[[149]](#cite_note-Colombian_Constitution_of_1991-157) In accordance with the principle of [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"), government is divided into three branches: the executive branch, the legislative branch and the judicial branch.[[194]](#cite_note-202)

As the head of the executive branch, the [President of Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia") serves as both [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), followed by the [Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Colombia "Vice President of Colombia") and the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Republic_of_Colombia "Council of Ministers of the Republic of Colombia"). The president is elected by popular vote to serve a single four-year term (In 2015, Colombia's Congress approved the repeal of a 2004 constitutional amendment that changed the one-term limit for presidents to a two-term limit).[[195]](#cite_note-203) At the provincial level executive power is vested in [department governors](/wiki/List_of_Colombian_Department_Governors "List of Colombian Department Governors"), [municipal mayors](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Colombia "Municipalities of Colombia") and local administrators for smaller administrative subdivisions, such as *[corregimientos](/wiki/Corregimientos_of_Colombia "Corregimientos of Colombia")* or *comunas*.[[196]](#cite_note-204) All regional elections are held one year and five months after the presidential election.[[197]](#cite_note-Government-205)[[198]](#cite_note-206)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Capitalio_National_de_Colombia%2C_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg/250px-Capitalio_National_de_Colombia%2C_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capitalio_National_de_Colombia,_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg)

[Capitolio Nacional](/wiki/Capitolio_Nacional "Capitolio Nacional"), seat of the [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_Colombia "Congress of Colombia")

The legislative branch of government is represented nationally by the [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_Colombia "Congress of Colombia"), a bicameral institution comprising a 166-seat [Chamber of Representatives](/wiki/Chamber_of_Representatives_of_Colombia "Chamber of Representatives of Colombia") and a 102-seat [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Colombia "Senate of Colombia").[[199]](#cite_note-207)[[200]](#cite_note-208) The Senate is elected nationally and the Chamber of Representatives is elected in electoral districts.[[201]](#cite_note-209) Members of both houses are elected to serve four-year terms two months before the president, also by popular vote.[[202]](#cite_note-210)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Palacio_de_Justicia_de_Colombia%2C_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg/250px-Palacio_de_Justicia_de_Colombia%2C_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palacio_de_Justicia_de_Colombia,_Bogot%C3%A1.jpg)

[Palace of Justice of Colombia](/wiki/Palace_of_Justice_of_Colombia "Palace of Justice of Colombia"), seat and symbol of the [Judiciary of Colombia](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Colombia "Judiciary of Colombia")

The judicial branch is headed by [four high courts](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Colombia "Judiciary of Colombia"),[[203]](#cite_note-211) consisting of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Justice_of_Colombia "Supreme Court of Justice of Colombia") which deals with penal and civil matters, the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Colombia) "Council of State (Colombia)"), which has special responsibility for [administrative law](/wiki/Administrative_law "Administrative law") and also provides legal advice to the executive, the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Colombia "Constitutional Court of Colombia"), responsible for assuring the integrity of the Colombian constitution, and the [Superior Council of Judicature](/wiki/Superior_Council_of_Judicature "Superior Council of Judicature"), responsible for auditing the judicial branch.[[204]](#cite_note-212) Colombia operates a system of [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)"), which since 1991 has been applied through an [adversarial system](/wiki/Adversarial_system "Adversarial system").[[205]](#cite_note-213)

Despite a number of controversies, the [democratic security policy](/wiki/Democratic_security_policy "Democratic security policy") has ensured that former President [Álvaro Uribe](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Uribe "Álvaro Uribe") remained popular among Colombian people, with his approval rating peaking at 76%, according to a poll in 2009.[[206]](#cite_note-214) However, having served two terms, he was constitutionally barred from seeking re-election in 2010.[[207]](#cite_note-215) In the run-off [elections](/wiki/2010_Colombian_presidential_election "2010 Colombian presidential election") on 20 June 2010 the former Minister of Defense [Juan Manuel Santos](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Santos "Juan Manuel Santos") won with 69% of the vote against the second most popular candidate, [Antanas Mockus](/wiki/Antanas_Mockus "Antanas Mockus"). A second round was required since no candidate received over the 50% winning threshold of votes.[[208]](#cite_note-216) Santos won re-election with nearly 51% of the vote in second-round [elections](/wiki/2014_Colombian_presidential_election "2014 Colombian presidential election") on 15 June 2014, beating right-wing rival [Óscar Iván Zuluaga](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Iv%C3%A1n_Zuluaga "Óscar Iván Zuluaga"), who won 45%.[[209]](#cite_note-217) In 2018, [Iván Duque](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Duque "Iván Duque") won in the second round of the [election](/wiki/2018_Colombian_presidential_election "2018 Colombian presidential election") with 54% of the vote, against 42% for his left-wing rival, [Gustavo Petro](/wiki/Gustavo_Petro "Gustavo Petro"). His term as Colombia's president ran for four years, beginning on 7 August 2018.[[210]](#cite_note-218) In 2022, Colombia [elected](/wiki/2022_Colombian_presidential_election "2022 Colombian presidential election") Gustavo Petro, who became its first leftist leader,[[211]](#cite_note-219) and [Francia Marquez](/wiki/Francia_Marquez "Francia Marquez"), who was the first black person elected as vice president.[[212]](#cite_note-220)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Departments of Colombia](/wiki/Departments_of_Colombia "Departments of Colombia") and [Municipalities of Colombia](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Colombia "Municipalities of Colombia")

See also: [List of cities in Colombia](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Colombia "List of cities in Colombia") and [Corregimientos of Colombia](/wiki/Corregimientos_of_Colombia "Corregimientos of Colombia")

Colombia is divided into 32 [departments](/wiki/Departments_of_Colombia "Departments of Colombia") and one [capital district](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá"), which is treated as a department (Bogotá also serves as the capital of the [department of Cundinamarca](/wiki/Cundinamarca_Department "Cundinamarca Department")). Departments are subdivided into [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Colombia "Municipalities of Colombia"), each of which is assigned a municipal seat, and municipalities are in turn subdivided into *[corregimientos](/wiki/Corregimientos_of_Colombia "Corregimientos of Colombia")* in rural areas and into *comunas* in urban areas. Each department has a local government with a governor and assembly directly elected to four-year terms, and each municipality is headed by a mayor and council. There is a popularly elected local administrative board in each of the *corregimientos* or *comunas*.[[213]](#cite_note-Administrative_divisions-221)[[214]](#cite_note-Concerning_the_territorial_organization-222)[[215]](#cite_note-223)[[216]](#cite_note-224)

In addition to the capital, four other cities have been designated [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Colombia "Districts of Colombia") (in effect special municipalities), on the basis of special distinguishing features. These are [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla"), [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia"), [Santa Marta](/wiki/Santa_Marta "Santa Marta") and [Buenaventura](/wiki/Buenaventura,_Colombia "Buenaventura, Colombia"). Some departments have local administrative subdivisions, where towns have a large concentration of population and municipalities are near each other (for example, in Antioquia and Cundinamarca). Where departments have a low population (for example Amazonas, Vaupés and Vichada), special administrative divisions are employed, such as "department *corregimientos*", which are a hybrid of a municipality and a *corregimiento*.[[213]](#cite_note-Administrative_divisions-221)[[214]](#cite_note-Concerning_the_territorial_organization-222)
